title: Känguru der Mathematik 2014 — Felix (Pré-Écolier, Grades 1-2)
source_organization: Känguru der Mathematik (Austria) / Kangourou sans Frontières
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper (kaenguru.at, via matematica.pt). For personal educational practice only.
answer_source: Official 2014_Answers.pdf answer table (Felix row), read and verified.

questions.question_text: The ladybird wants to sit on a flower with five petals and a stem with three leaves. On which flower should it sit?
questions.solution: Find the flower that has exactly 5 petals and a stem with 3 leaves. Flower B matches both.
solution_steps: Look for 5 petals on the flower.
Then check the stem for 3 leaves.
Flower B has both, so B.

questions.question_text: Theresa moves a pencil along the line, starting at the arrow. In which order will she pass the shapes?
questions.solution: Trace the line from the arrow and note each shape as you reach it. The order is triangle, then square, then circle.
solution_steps: Start at the arrow and follow the curvy line.
Write down each shape as you pass it.
The order is triangle, square, circle.

questions.question_text: There are more grey squares than white. How many more?
questions.solution: In the 5×5 grid there are 17 grey squares (the border plus the centre) and 8 white squares. The difference is 17 − 8 = 9.
solution_steps: Count grey squares: 17 (border + centre).
Count white squares: 8.
Difference = 17 − 8 = 9.

questions.question_text: A big square is made from 25 small squares. A few small squares have been lost. How many have been lost?
questions.solution: Count the small squares still present (15) and subtract from 25. So 25 − 15 = 10 squares were lost.
solution_steps: A full square needs 25 small squares.
Count the squares still there: 15.

questions.question_text: Put the animals in order of size, beginning with the smallest. Which animal will be in the middle?
questions.solution: Ordering by size from smallest to largest gives squirrel (1), monkey (5), tiger (2), ostrich (4), giraffe (3). The middle one, third in the row, is the tiger — animal 2.
solution_steps: Smallest to largest: squirrel(1), monkey(5), tiger(2), ostrich(4), giraffe(3).
The middle position is the 3rd one.
That is the tiger, animal 2.

questions.question_text: How many ducks weigh the same as a crocodile?
questions.solution: One crocodile balances 2 lions, and 1 lion balances 2 ducks. So a crocodile equals 2 lions = 2 × 2 = 4 ducks.
solution_steps: 1 crocodile = 2 lions (first scale).
1 lion = 2 ducks (second scale).
Crocodile = 2 × 2 = 4 ducks.

questions.question_text: The kangaroo is inside how many circles?
questions.solution: Check each of the four circles to see whether the kangaroo lies inside it. The kangaroo is inside 3 of the circles.
solution_steps: Take each circle one at a time.
See if the kangaroo is inside it.
It is inside 3 circles.

questions.question_text: Following the arrows → 2, ↓ 2, → 3, ↑ 3, → 2, ↑ 2 from home, which animal does the ant reach?
questions.solution: Move on the grid step by step: right 2, down 2, right 3, up 3, right 2, up 2. The ant lands on the square with the butterfly.
solution_steps: Follow each arrow one square at a time from home.
Net move: 7 squares right and 3 squares up.
The ant lands on the butterfly.

questions.question_text: Max cuts a rectangle into two pieces. One piece has a zig-zag (three-peak) top. What does the other piece look like?
questions.solution: The two pieces must fit back together into the straight-edged rectangle, so the second piece's top must be the matching zig-zag that fills the gaps. That is piece E.
solution_steps: The two pieces rejoin into a full rectangle.
The second top must fill exactly where the first zig-zag dips.
That matching piece is E.

questions.question_text: Seven sticks lie on top of each other. Stick 2 is at the very bottom and stick 6 is on top. Which stick lies exactly in the middle?
questions.solution: Work out the stacking order from which stick covers which. With 7 sticks, the middle one is the 4th from the bottom, which turns out to be stick 3.
solution_steps: Find the top-to-bottom order from the overlaps (2 bottom, 6 top).
The middle of 7 is the 4th stick from the bottom.
That stick is number 3.

questions.question_text: How many numbers that use only the digits 1, 2 or 3 are bigger than 10 and smaller than 32? Digits may repeat.
questions.solution: Two-digit numbers using only 1, 2, 3 that are between 11 and 31: 11, 12, 13, 21, 22, 23, 31. That is 7 numbers.
solution_steps: The numbers are two digits, each digit 1, 2 or 3, and between 11 and 31.
That makes 7 numbers.

questions.question_text: The rabbit family eats either 10 carrots or 2 cabbages each day. Last week they ate 6 cabbages in total. How many carrots did they eat last week?
questions.solution: Eating 6 cabbages at 2 per cabbage-day means 3 cabbage-days. The other 7 − 3 = 4 days were carrot days at 10 each, giving 4 × 10 = 40 carrots.
solution_steps: 6 cabbages ÷ 2 per day = 3 cabbage-days.
Remaining days: 7 − 3 = 4 carrot-days.
Carrots = 4 × 10 = 40.

questions.question_text: A square is cut into four pieces. Which shape can you NOT make with these four pieces?
questions.solution: Try arranging the four pieces to form each shape without gaps or overlaps. Shape D cannot be built from the four pieces.
solution_steps: Use all four pieces, no gaps or overlaps.
Test each target shape.
Only shape D cannot be made.

questions.question_text: Each of the digits 2, 3, 4 and 5 is placed in a square to make two 2-digit numbers, which are then added. What is the biggest sum they could make?
questions.solution: To make the sum biggest, put the two largest digits (5 and 4) in the tens places and the smaller ones (3 and 2) in the units: 53 + 42 = 95.
solution_steps: Largest digits go in the tens places: 5 and 4.
Smaller digits go in the units: 3 and 2.

questions.question_text: Ingrid has 4 red, 3 blue, 2 green and 1 yellow cube. She builds the object so that cubes of the same colour never touch. Which colour is the cube with the question mark?

options.E: This cannot be worked out for certain.

questions.solution: Place the colours so none of the same touch. Red is the most common (4 cubes), and the only way to keep reds apart forces the question-mark cube to be red.
solution_steps: Same colours may never touch.
With 4 reds to spread out, their positions are forced.
The question-mark cube must be red.
